name: Sport & Games
description: Sports math, game theory, and probability — from the rugby field to the poker table!
author: Game Night Crew

questions.question: In rugby, a converted try is worth how many points in total?
questions.explanation: A try is worth 5 points and the conversion kick adds 2 more, giving you 7 points total. Rugby 101!

questions.question: How many players are on a soccer team on the field at one time?
questions.explanation: 11 players per side, including the goalkeeper. If you got this wrong, you might need to watch more Bafana Bafana!

questions.question: A cricket innings has a maximum of how many overs in a standard ODI match?
questions.explanation: 50 overs per side in a One Day International. That's 300 balls to bat or bowl — plenty of action!

questions.question: If a Springbok rugby team scores 3 tries (all converted) and 2 penalty kicks, what's the total score?
questions.explanation: 3 converted tries: 3 x 7 = 21. Two penalties: 2 x 3 = 6. Total: 21 + 6 = 27. A solid day at the office for the Boks!

questions.question: In a game of pool (8-ball), how many balls are on the table at the start (including the cue ball)?
questions.explanation: 15 object balls plus 1 cue ball = 16 balls total. Now rack 'em up!

questions.question: A cricket batsman scores 45 runs off 30 balls. What is their strike rate?
questions.explanation: Strike rate = (runs / balls) x 100 = (45/30) x 100 = 150. That's aggressive batting — T20 style!

questions.question: In rugby, how many players are on one team during a match (on the field)?
questions.explanation: 15 players per side in rugby union. 8 forwards and 7 backs. The Springboks wouldn't have it any other way!

questions.question: If Bafana Bafana play 10 games, win 4, draw 3, and lose 3, how many points do they have (3 for win, 1 for draw)?
questions.explanation: Wins: 4 x 3 = 12. Draws: 3 x 1 = 3. Total: 15 points. Not a bad run for Bafana!

questions.question: A bowler takes 3 wickets for 28 runs in 7 overs. What is their bowling average in this spell?
questions.explanation: Bowling average = runs conceded / wickets taken = 28 / 3 = 9.33. A tidy spell of bowling!

questions.question: In a standard deck of cards, what's the probability of drawing a heart?
questions.explanation: 13 hearts out of 52 cards = 13/52 = 1/4. Each suit gets an equal quarter of the deck!

questions.question: A runner completes the Comrades Marathon (approximately 90 km) in 9 hours. What was their average speed in km/h?
questions.explanation: 90 km / 9 hours = 10 km/h. That's a solid Comrades pace — not easy to maintain over that distance between Durban and Pietermaritzburg!

questions.question: In a T20 cricket match, a team needs 48 runs from the last 4 overs. What required run rate do they need per over?
questions.explanation: 48 runs / 4 overs = 12 runs per over. That's two sixes an over — gotta go big or go home!

questions.question: If you roll two dice, what is the probability of getting a total of 7?
questions.explanation: There are 6 ways to make 7 (1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1) out of 36 possible outcomes. 6/36 = 1/6. Seven is the most likely total when rolling two dice!

questions.question: A rugby goal kicker has a 75% success rate. If he attempts 8 kicks in a match, how many conversions would you expect him to make?
questions.explanation: Expected value: 8 x 0.75 = 6 conversions. Handre Pollard would approve!

questions.question: Arrange these cricket dismissal types from MOST to LEAST common in professional cricket: (1) Bowled (2) Caught (3) Run out (4) LBW
questions.explanation: Caught is by far the most common (over 50%), followed by LBW, then bowled, then run out. Catching wins matches — every coach knows that!

questions.question: In a round-robin tournament with 8 teams (each team plays every other team once), how many total matches are played?
questions.explanation: Formula: n(n-1)/2 = 8 x 7 / 2 = 28 matches. That's a lot of weekends at the sports ground!

questions.question: A soccer goalkeeper saves 70% of penalties. In a shootout of 5 penalties, how many goals would you statistically expect to go in (multiply by 10 to give a whole number, e.g. 1.5 = 15)?
questions.explanation: If the keeper saves 70%, then 30% go in. 5 x 0.30 = 1.5 goals expected, so answer is 15. In reality, shootout conversion rates are much higher — pressure changes everything!

questions.question: Wayde van Niekerk's 400m world record is 43.03 seconds. What was his approximate average speed in km/h?
questions.explanation: 400m in 43.03s = 9.30 m/s. Converting: 9.30 x 3.6 = 33.5 km/h. That SA legend was flying in Rio 2016!

questions.question: In poker, you're dealt 2 cards from a standard deck. What's the probability of getting a pocket pair (both cards the same rank)?
questions.explanation: After receiving any first card, there are 3 matching cards left out of 51 remaining. Probability: 3/51 = 1/17 ≈ 5.9%, about 6%. You'll get dealt a pocket pair roughly once every 17 hands!

questions.question: A cricket team's Net Run Rate (NRR) is calculated as (runs scored per over) minus (runs conceded per over). Team A scored 280 in 50 overs and conceded 260 in 48 overs. What is their NRR (multiply by 100 and round to nearest whole number, e.g. 0.18 = 18)?
questions.explanation: Scored per over: 280/50 = 5.60. Conceded per over: 260/48 = 5.42. NRR: 5.60 - 5.42 = +0.18, so answer is 18. NRR is the tiebreaker that keeps cricket fans doing maths!

questions.question: The Springboks won the 2019 Rugby World Cup final 32-12 against England. If all their points came from tries (7 pts each) and penalties (3 pts each), and they scored 2 tries, how many penalties did they kick?
questions.explanation: 2 tries x 7 = 14 points. Remaining: 32 - 14 = 18 points from penalties. 18 / 3 = 6 penalties. Handre Pollard's boot was golden that day!

questions.question: In a best-of-5 tennis match, what is the maximum number of sets that can be played?
questions.explanation: Best of 5 means first to 3 sets wins. Maximum is 3-2, so 5 sets total. Marathon matches at Grand Slams can last for hours!

questions.question: You flip a fair coin 4 times. What's the probability of getting exactly 3 heads?
questions.explanation: Ways to get exactly 3 heads from 4 flips: C(4,3) = 4. Total outcomes: 2^4 = 16. Probability: 4/16 = 1/4. The binomial distribution in action!

questions.question: A marathon is 42.195 km. If a runner wants to finish in under 3 hours, what minimum average pace (minutes per km) must they maintain?
questions.explanation: 3 hours = 180 minutes. 180 / 42.195 = 4.27 min/km ≈ 4:16 per km. Sub-3 marathon is an elite goal — you need serious speed!

questions.question: In a Lotto draw, you pick 6 numbers from 1-52. How many possible combinations are there?
questions.explanation: C(52,6) = 52! / (6! x 46!) = 20,358,520 ≈ 20 million. Your odds of winning the SA Lotto are roughly 1 in 20 million. Rather save that R5!

questions.question: In the Duckworth-Lewis method (simplified), a team batting second in a rain-affected ODI has used 30 of 50 overs and lost 4 wickets. Approximately what percentage of their resources remain?
questions.explanation: With 20 overs left and 6 wickets in hand, roughly 30% of resources remain according to the DLS tables. Rain and cricket maths — the DLS method is beautifully complex!

questions.question: A rugby team wins 60% of their lineouts and 55% of their scrums. If they have 20 lineouts and 12 scrums in a match, how many set pieces do they expect to win in total?
questions.explanation: Lineouts won: 20 x 0.60 = 12. Scrums won: 12 x 0.55 = 6.6. Total: 12 + 6.6 = 18.6, rounded to 19. Set piece dominance wins Test matches!

questions.question: A cricket batsman averages 52.5 over 80 innings (40 not outs). How many total career runs has he scored?
questions.explanation: Batting average = total runs / (innings - not outs). So: 52.5 = runs / (80 - 40) = runs / 40. Runs = 52.5 x 40 = 2 100. Cricket averages can be tricky — not outs are the key!

questions.question: In a game of darts, you need exactly 170 to check out (the highest possible). Which combination achieves this?
questions.explanation: Triple 20 (60) + Triple 20 (60) + Bullseye (50) = 170. The legendary 'Big Fish' checkout — the holy grail of darts!

questions.question: Rank these sports by the number of players on a team (on field/court), FEWEST to MOST: (1) Basketball (2) Rugby Union (3) Water Polo (4) Cricket
questions.explanation: Basketball: 5, Water polo: 7, Cricket: 11, Rugby union: 15. From 5 to 15 — quite the range in team sizes!

questions.question: In the 2023 Rugby World Cup, South Africa beat New Zealand 12-11 in the final. If all SA's points came from penalties (3 pts each), how many penalties did Pollard kick?
questions.explanation: 12 points / 3 per penalty = 4 penalties. No tries needed — Pollard's boot and the Bok defence did the job. Ugly? Maybe. Effective? Absolutely!

questions.question: A bowler's economy rate is 4.2 runs per over. If he bowls his full allocation of 10 overs in an ODI, how many runs does he concede?
questions.explanation: 4.2 runs/over x 10 overs = 42 runs. That's a very tidy spell — the kind of bowling that wins tournaments!

questions.question: You're playing Texas Hold'em. The flop shows 3 cards of the same suit, and you hold 2 cards of that suit (giving you a flush). What approximate percentage of hands at a 9-player table would also have a flush draw (at least 2 of that suit)?
questions.explanation: With 5 of 13 suited cards accounted for (3 on board + 2 in your hand), there are 8 remaining among the other 16 player cards plus 45 unknown. Roughly 20% of opponents could have 2+ of that suit. Always be cautious with a made flush on a monotone board!

questions.question: In a single-elimination tournament with 64 teams, how many total matches are played to determine a winner?
questions.explanation: In single elimination, every team except the winner loses exactly once. So 64 - 1 = 63 matches. This shortcut works for any size tournament!

questions.question: A soccer team has a 40% chance of winning any given match. What's the probability (as a percentage, rounded to nearest whole number) they win 3 matches in a row?
questions.explanation: 0.40 x 0.40 x 0.40 = 0.064 = 6.4%, rounded to 6%. Winning streaks are harder to maintain than you think!

questions.question: In Test cricket, a team declares at 450/6 after 120 overs on day 2. The run rate was 3.75/over. If they want to bowl the opposition out twice, and the average Test innings lasts 80 overs, do they have enough time in the remaining 3 days (90 overs/day)?
questions.explanation: Remaining overs in match: 3 days x 90 = 270 overs. Need: 2 x 80 = 160 overs to bowl. 270 - 160 = 110 overs to spare. Plenty of time — that's why declarations are an art form in Test cricket!

questions.question: In Monopoly, using two dice, what is the most likely number of spaces you'll move? (The statistical mode)
questions.explanation: With two dice, 7 has the most combinations (1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1 = 6 ways out of 36). So 7 is the mode. That's why properties 7 spaces from Jail are so valuable!

questions.question: A rugby team's points-scoring follows a Poisson distribution with a mean of 24 points per game. What's the approximate probability they score exactly 24 points in a match?
questions.explanation: For Poisson with lambda=24, P(X=24) = (e^-24 x 24^24) / 24! ≈ 0.081 ≈ 8%. Even the most likely single outcome has a surprisingly low probability with large lambda values!

questions.question: In the FIFA World Cup group stage (4 teams, each plays 3 games), what is the maximum number of points a team can have and still be eliminated?
questions.explanation: If 3 teams each beat the 4th team and beat/lose to each other in a cycle, you can have 3 teams on 6 points. With goal difference as tiebreaker, one team on 6 points goes home. Brutal!

questions.question: Rank these poker hands from WEAKEST to STRONGEST: (1) Full House (2) Flush (3) Straight (4) Four of a Kind
questions.explanation: Straight < Flush < Full House < Four of a Kind. The ranking is based on rarity — a four of a kind is much harder to get than a straight. Know your poker hand rankings!

questions.question: In the Monty Hall problem, you pick a door, the host opens another door showing a goat, and you're offered to switch. What's your probability of winning the car if you switch?
questions.explanation: Switching gives you a 2/3 chance of winning! Your initial pick had 1/3 probability. When the host reveals a goat, the other door absorbs the remaining 2/3. Counter-intuitive but mathematically proven!

questions.question: In a rugby season, a team plays 14 matches. They need a minimum of 40 log points to qualify for playoffs (4 for a win, 2 for draw, 0 for loss, plus 1 bonus point for 4+ tries or losing by 7 or fewer). What is the minimum number of wins needed if they get NO bonus points and no draws?
questions.explanation: 40 points / 4 per win = 10 wins minimum with no bonus points. In reality, bonus points are crucial — they're the difference between making playoffs and going home!

questions.question: A cricket team chasing 300 in 50 overs is 180/4 after 30 overs. Using the DLS method (simplified: resources remaining with 6 wickets and 20 overs ≈ 40.5%), what's their par score at this point?
questions.explanation: Resources used ≈ 100% - 40.5% = 59.5%. Par score = 300 x 0.595 = 178.5 ≈ 179. They're at 180, so just barely ahead! DLS keeps cricket fair in rain delays.

questions.question: In game theory, the Nash Equilibrium of the penalty kick game (kicker vs goalkeeper) suggests what strategy?
options: Always kick to the keeper's weaker side
Mix directions randomly with specific probabilities
questions.explanation: The Nash Equilibrium is a mixed strategy — randomise your kicks with probabilities based on each player's success rates in each direction. Predictability is death in penalty kicks!

questions.question: In a chess tournament with 10 players in a round-robin format (everyone plays everyone once), how many total games are played?
questions.explanation: C(10,2) = 10 x 9 / 2 = 45 games. The combinatorics formula n(n-1)/2 is your friend for round-robin calculations!

questions.question: The Birthday Paradox: in a rugby squad of 23 players, what's the approximate probability that at least two players share a birthday?
questions.explanation: With 23 people, there's approximately a 50.7% chance of a shared birthday! It seems impossibly high, but with 253 possible pairs (C(23,2)), the odds stack up fast. Counter-intuitive probability at its finest!

questions.question: In a standard 52-card deck, how many possible 5-card poker hands are there? Give your answer in thousands (e.g., 2598 for 2,598,960).
questions.explanation: C(52,5) = 52! / (5! x 47!) = 2,598,960 ≈ 2,599 thousand. Nearly 2.6 million possible hands — and only 4 of them are royal flushes!

questions.question: A cricket team's batting order has 11 players. If the top 4 are fixed, in how many ways can the remaining 7 batsmen be arranged?
questions.explanation: 7! = 7 x 6 x 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 5 040 possible arrangements. And coaches agonize over every single one!

questions.question: In the Prisoner's Dilemma (iterated game), which strategy famously won Axelrod's tournament?
questions.explanation: Tit for Tat — start by cooperating, then mirror your opponent's last move. Simple, forgiving, and retaliatory. It won because it promotes cooperation while punishing betrayal. Game theory gold!

questions.question: A sprinter's reaction time is 0.15 seconds and they run 100m in 10.00 seconds (total time including reaction). What is their actual running speed in m/s (multiply by 100 for whole number, e.g. 10.15 = 1015)?
questions.explanation: Running time = 10.00 - 0.15 = 9.85 seconds for 100m. Speed = 100/9.85 = 10.15 m/s, so answer is 1015. Those hundredths of a second in reaction time make all the difference at the Olympics!

questions.question: In cricket's Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method, which mathematical function best describes the 'resources remaining' curve as a function of overs and wickets?
questions.explanation: The DLS method uses an exponential decay function where the decay rate depends on wickets lost: R(u,w) = Z(w) x (1 - exp(-b(w) x u)). The parameters change with wickets, making it a family of exponential curves. Frank Duckworth was a proper stats genius!

questions.question: In a double round-robin league with 16 teams (each team plays every other team twice), and 3 points for a win, 1 for a draw, 0 for a loss — what is the maximum possible points for the league champion?
questions.explanation: Each team plays 15 opponents x 2 = 30 matches. Maximum: 30 wins x 3 points = 90 points. A perfect season — theoretically possible but practically unheard of in any real league!

questions.question: According to the Bradley-Terry model in sports statistics, if Team A has a strength parameter of 3.0 and Team B has 1.5, what is the probability of Team A winning a head-to-head match?
questions.explanation: P(A beats B) = strength_A / (strength_A + strength_B) = 3.0 / (3.0 + 1.5) = 3.0 / 4.5 = 0.667 = 67%. The Bradley-Terry model is the foundation of many sports ranking systems!

questions.question: In chess, the Elo rating system says a player with a 200-point advantage has an expected score of approximately what against their opponent?
questions.explanation: The Elo formula: E = 1 / (1 + 10^(-d/400)). With d=200: E = 1/(1 + 10^(-0.5)) = 1/(1 + 0.316) = 1/1.316 = 0.760 ≈ 76%. The Elo system — invented by Arpad Elo, used everywhere from chess to online gaming!

questions.question: Rank these by the number of possible legal game states (FEWEST to MOST): (1) Tic-Tac-Toe (2) Chess (3) Checkers (4) Go (19x19 board)
questions.explanation: Tic-Tac-Toe: ~5,478 states. Checkers: ~5 x 10^20. Chess: ~10^47. Go: ~2 x 10^170 (more than atoms in the universe!). Go's complexity is why AI took so much longer to master it than chess.
